Client 2: Net, we’re probably like… seventy-ish?
Client 1: That’s net, Robert. Gross is higher—about ninety-two thousand a year, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect catch—gross, not take-home. So about $91,800 a year gross household income?
Client 1: Yes, $91,800 gross for the household.
Advisor: Got it—one sec, I’m just writing that down. And Jennifer, that’s coming from a few sources, right—some business income, some Social Security, and a bonus?

Client 1: Right—he’s basically inactive work-wise, but there’s still that income stream.
Advisor: Understood. Okay, shifting to expenses—going back to what you said about obligations, Jennifer—when you add up your normal monthly spending, what’s your total monthly expenses number, roughly?
Client 1: About $2,700 a month for our baseline expenses.
Advisor: $2,700 per month—okay. Does that include debt payments like a car loan or credit cards, or is that separate?
Client 2: That includes the mortgage—

Client 2: Net, we’re probably like… seventy-ish?
Client 1: That’s net, Robert. Gross is higher—about ninety-two thousand a year, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect catch—gross, not take-home. So about $91,800 a year gross household income?
Client 1: Yes, $91,800 gross for the household.
Advisor: Got it—one sec, I’m just writing that down. And Jennifer, that’s coming from a few sources, right—some business income, some Social Security, and a bonus?

Advisor: Now assets—this is the other big driver. When you think investable assets—cash, brokerage, retirement accounts—what’s the total you’re working with across both of you?
Client 2: Like… six hundred thousand?
Client 1: It’s higher. Closer to $688,350 total investable assets.
Advisor: Okay—$688,350 investable assets total. Let’s break that down so I don’t mix buckets. Jennifer, you have a taxable account at a bank—about $441,400, right?
Client 1: Yes, about $441,400 in the taxable bank account.

Advisor: Totally fair—liquidity matters, especially with conservative preferences. We’ll come back to that. Any debts at all—mortgage, HELOC, car note—anything outstanding?
Client 1: No, no liabilities. We don’t owe on the house or anything like that.
Advisor: Great. Speaking of the house—just to make sure we cover it—your primary residence value is about $411,500?
Client 1: Yes, about $411,500.
Advisor: And protection planning—Jennifer, you have long-term care coverage. What’s the monthly cost on that policy?

Client 2: Net, we’re probably like… seventy-ish?
Client 1: That’s net, Robert. Gross is higher—about ninety-two thousand a year, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect catch—gross, not take-home. So about $91,800 a year gross household income?
Client 1: Yes, $91,800 gross for the household.
Advisor: Got it—one sec, I’m just writing that down. And Jennifer, that’s coming from a few sources, right—some business income, some Social Security, and a bonus?

Client 2: Net, we’re probably like… seventy-ish?
Client 1: That’s net, Robert. Gross is higher—about ninety-two thousand a year, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect catch—gross, not take-home. So about $91,800 a year gross household income?

Client 2: Net, we’re probably like… seventy-ish?
Client 1: That’s net, Robert. Gross is higher—about ninety-two thousand a year, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect catch—gross, not take-home. So about $91,800 a year gross household income?
Client 1: Yes, $91,800 gross for the household.
Advisor: Got it—one sec, I’m just writing that down. And Jennifer, that’s coming from a few sources, right—some business income, some Social Security, and a bonus?

Client 2: Yeah, U.S. only.
Advisor: Perfect.
Advisor: One more admin-y thing, and then we’ll get back into the accounts: are you working with a U.S. RIA—like a registered investment advisor setup—for your planning and investments, or is it more brokerage-only?
Client 2: Wait, what’s the difference again—RIA versus broker?
Advisor: Yeah—good question. RIA is the advisory relationship—ongoing advice and management—versus just placing trades. The way we’re set up here is RIA-style. Does that match how you think about it?

Client 2: Yeah, about $73,250. That one’s mine.
Client 1: And that’s the one I don’t love because it’s not very liquid.
Advisor: Totally fair—liquidity matters, especially with conservative preferences. We’ll come back to that. Any debts at all—mortgage, HELOC, car note—anything outstanding?
Client 1: No, no liabilities. We don’t owe on the house or anything like that.
Advisor: Great. Speaking of the house—just to make sure we cover it—your primary residence value is about $411,500?

Client 2: Yeah, traffic was a mess, but we’re good.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep this practical. Jennifer, do you want to start—what prompted you two to come in now?
Client 1: I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything, especially with my obligations and retirement getting closer.
Client 2: And I want the simple version. I get lost when it gets too… spreadsheet-y.
Advisor: Fair. We’ll do plain English. Quick housekeeping: just confirming, Jennifer you’re “Client 1” and Robert you’re “Client 2,” right?

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi Robert—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how’s your day going so far?
Client 1: Busy, honestly. I came straight from the office, so my brain’s still in work mode.
Client 2: Yeah, traffic was a mess, but we’re good.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep this practical. Jennifer, do you want to start—what prompted you two to come in now?

Client 2: Net, we’re probably like… seventy-ish?
Client 1: That’s net, Robert. Gross is higher—about ninety-two thousand a year, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect catch—gross, not take-home. So about $91,800 a year gross household income?
Client 1: Yes, $91,800 gross for the household.
Advisor: Got it—one sec, I’m just writing that down. And Jennifer, that’s coming from a few sources, right—some business income, some Social Security, and a bonus?

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi Robert—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how’s your day going so far?
Client 1: Busy, honestly. I came straight from the office, so my brain’s still in work mode.
Client 2: Yeah, traffic was a mess, but we’re good.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep this practical. Jennifer, do you want to start—what prompted you two to come in now?
Client 1: I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything, especially with my obligations and retirement getting closer.

Client 2: Yeah, we’re married—second marriage.
Client 1: Second marriage for both of us, actually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you for correcting that. So “secondly wedded,” basically.
Client 2: Right.
Advisor: And then taxes—when you said earlier you worry about surprises, I want to confirm the federal bracket you typically land in. Is it around twenty-four percent?

Client 1: No—Robert, it’s twenty-four. We bumped up.
Client 2: Oh—right, right. I mix those up.
Advisor: Totally fair—those brackets are easy to blur. So we’ll use 24% as the working federal bracket.
Client 1: Yes, 24%.
Advisor: Quick clarification while we’re on it—when you say “24%,” you’re talking about the marginal bracket, not that you pay 24% of everything. Does that sound like what you mean?

Client 2: I thought it was closer to like, seventy?
Client 1: No—Robert, that’s when you add the bonus and Social Security. My gross from work is about sixty-one.
Advisor: Okay, thank you—so Jennifer, gross annual income from employment is about $60,800-ish, and then there are other income lines on top of that. Is that fair?
Client 1: Yes—$60,800 gross for me, from working.
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just writing that down.

Client 2: Yeah—sounds good.
Advisor: Alright. Going back to what you said earlier about not wanting surprises—Jennifer, I want to anchor your work income piece so we’re not hand-waving it. Are you still actively working right now, or fully retired?
Client 1: I’m still working—yeah, employed. I’m running the business day to day.
Advisor: Got it—still employed. And when we talk income for you, Jennifer, what’s your gross annual from work, roughly? Before taxes and all that.
Client 1: It’s about sixty thousand a year.

Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just writing that down.
Advisor: And Jennifer, I’m going to ask a slightly annoying detail: your date of birth. Month, day, year—just so we’re not guessing on Social Security timing and required distributions later.
Client 1: May 20th, 1961.
Advisor: May 20, 1961—thank you.
Client 2: Wait, isn’t she 1960? I always mess that up.

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi Robert—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how’s your day going so far?
Client 1: Busy, honestly. I came straight from the office, so my brain’s still in work mode.
Client 2: Yeah, traffic was a mess, but we’re good.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep this practical. Jennifer, do you want to start—what prompted you two to come in now?

Advisor: Totally fair—liquidity matters, especially with conservative preferences. We’ll come back to that. Any debts at all—mortgage, HELOC, car note—anything outstanding?
Client 1: No, no liabilities. We don’t owe on the house or anything like that.

Advisor: And protection planning—Jennifer, you have long-term care coverage. What’s the monthly cost on that policy?

Client 2: Yes, spouse/partner.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Let me jot that down—one sec.

Client 2: Well—she’s also on payroll though.

Client 1: Yes, that’s about right.

Client 2: Yes, spouse/partner.
